question: What techniques exist for self-improving agents without fine-tuning?
answer:
## Answer
Without fine-tuning, self-improving agents usually improve **at test time** by changing prompts, memory, search state, or tool-use behavior rather than model weights. The main families of techniques are: **reflection/memory**, **iterative self-feedback and revision**, **reasoning+acting with external tools**, **search over multiple reasoning branches**, **self-consistency over sampled solutions**, and **curriculum/skill-library accumulation**. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366?utm_source=openai))

- **Reflection + episodic memory:** the agent critiques its own past behavior, stores the reflection, and reuses it on later attempts. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366?utm_source=openai))
- **Iterative self-feedback / rewrite:** the same model generates an answer, critiques it, and revises it in a loop, with no extra training. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17651?utm_source=openai))
- **Reasoning + acting:** the agent alternates between internal reasoning and external actions (APIs, environments, tools) to gather new information and update plans. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629?utm_source=openai))
- **Search / backtracking over thoughts:** the agent branches over candidate reasoning steps, evaluates them, and can backtrack when a branch looks weak. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.10601?utm_source=openai))
- **Self-consistency:** the agent samples multiple reasoning paths and selects the most consistent final answer. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.11171?utm_source=openai))
- **Curriculum + skill library:** the agent accumulates reusable skills and follows an automatic curriculum, improving via black-box prompting rather than fine-tuning. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.16291?utm_source=openai))

## Sources
- Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning — https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366?utm_source=openai))
- Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback — https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17651 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.17651?utm_source=openai))
- ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models — https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.03629?utm_source=openai))
- Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models — https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.10601 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.10601?utm_source=openai))
- Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models — https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.11171 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2203.11171?utm_source=openai))
- Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models — https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.16291 ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.16291?utm_source=openai))

## Limitations
This is a synthesis from a small set of primary LLM-agent papers, so it is not an exhaustive taxonomy. The papers use different terms for overlapping ideas, and the reported gains are benchmark-specific rather than a guarantee of general self-improvement. I also omitted one analysis paper on intrinsic self-correction because it evaluates the idea rather than introducing a distinct technique. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366?utm_source=openai))

subquestions: What does 'self-improving' mean when model weights are not updated?
Which inference-time techniques let an agent improve across attempts?
How do these techniques differ from fine-tuning or RL?
What are representative primary sources for each technique?
required_aspects: Techniques for improvement at test time
expected_source_types: Official project pages or code repos when needed

rationale_per_query: Find a canonical paper for reflection/memory-based self-improvement without weight updates.
Find a canonical paper for iterative critique-and-revision without fine-tuning.
Find a canonical paper for tool-use + environment-feedback loops.
Find a canonical paper for search/backtracking over reasoning paths.

accepted_sources.title: Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning
accepted_sources.reason: Directly about a no-finetuning self-improvement loop using linguistic feedback and episodic memory.

accepted_sources.title: Self-Refine: Iterative Refinement with Self-Feedback
accepted_sources.reason: Directly about iterative self-critique and revision without extra training.

accepted_sources.title: ReAct: Synergizing Reasoning and Acting in Language Models
accepted_sources.reason: Directly about reasoning+acting loops that improve decisions through external feedback.

accepted_sources.title: Tree of Thoughts: Deliberate Problem Solving with Large Language Models
accepted_sources.reason: Directly about search/backtracking over multiple candidate reasoning paths.

accepted_sources.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models
accepted_sources.reason: Directly about sampling multiple reasoning paths and selecting the most consistent answer.

accepted_sources.title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
accepted_sources.reason: Directly about black-box agent improvement via curriculum, skill library, and iterative prompting.

rejected_sources.title: Large Language Models have Intrinsic Self-Correction Ability

rejection_reasons: Useful as an evaluation/critique paper, but it is not itself a primary technique proposal for self-improving agents; it is less directly actionable for answering 'what techniques exist'.

evidence_table.claim: Self-improving agents can be built without fine-tuning by using test-time loops that change prompts, memory, search state, or tool use instead of model weights.
Reflexion improves agents by having them verbally reflect on feedback and store reflections in episodic memory for later trials, rather than updating weights.
Self-Refine uses the same LLM as generator, feedback provider, and refiner to iteratively improve outputs without supervised data, extra training, or RL.
ReAct interleaves reasoning traces with task actions so the agent can gather external information and update plans during execution.
Tree of Thoughts improves problem solving by exploring multiple coherent reasoning branches and backtracking/self-evaluating choices.
Self-Consistency improves reasoning by sampling diverse reasoning paths and selecting the most consistent answer.

Voyager improves capability in a black-box setting via an automatic curriculum, an ever-growing skill library, and iterative prompting that uses environment feedback, execution errors, and self-verification.

contradiction_audit.resolution: No direct conflicts among the accepted sources. The techniques differ mainly in mechanism: some revise a single draft (Self-Refine), some store reflections (Reflexion), some search over branches (Tree of Thoughts), and some aggregate multiple samples (Self-Consistency).
uncertainty_notes: Terminology is not standardized across papers; several techniques overlap in practice.
Reported gains are benchmark-specific and do not guarantee general self-improvement.

supported_claims: Self-improvement without fine-tuning is possible at test time.
Reflection and episodic memory are a documented technique.
Iterative self-feedback/revision is a documented technique.
Reasoning+acting loops with tools are a documented technique.
Search/backtracking over thoughts is a documented technique.
Self-consistency over multiple sampled reasoning paths is a documented technique.
Curriculum plus skill-library accumulation is a documented technique.
